record.text: Before you sign up for a new AI platform, check out what your current vendors provide. Look at Copilot in Microsoft 365, Gemini in Google Workspace, and HubSpot’s AI tools. These features are already part of contracts and security models you know. Having that context

Data control is a real issue. It’s not about avoiding AI altogether. It’s about using tools that integrate with what you already have—like Microsoft, Google, or your CRM—and starting with specific features. This way, you remain in control while gaining an advantage.

Most funnels don’t leak at the top. They tend to leak in the middle when follow-ups aren’t quick or frequent enough. If leads have to wait days for a reply, they’re likely to move on. AI shines here because it ensures timely nudges. That’s what really matters.

record.text: If Deep Seek were going to do it, it would’ve. It, and “Local” or “Open” Models, are available *now* and people still pay for ChatGPT and Claude. Those options aren’t as good — whatever that means to LLM users — and making them better will make them cost more.

One other note from today's newsletter: I'm worried about over-use of LLMs in software engineering. If people aren't reading (or are barely reading) code they ship, "writing code fast" becomes a liability. It also can stop you from learning to solve problems.
www.wheresyoured.at/why-are-we-s...

images.alt:
I also want to be clear that I am extremely nervous about how many people appear to be fine with not reading code. I am currently (very slowly) learning Python, and every new thing I learn reinforces my overwhelming anxiety that there is a lot of software being written today by people who don’t read the output from LLMs and, in some cases, may not have understood it if they did. While I’m not saying all or even many software engineers might do this, I am alarmed by the idea that it’s becoming more commonplace — and even more alarmed that the reaction appears to be “ah it’s fine who gives a shit, it works.” 

Guess what! It doesn’t always work. Amazon Web Services had multiple recent outages caused by use of its Kiro AI coding tool, and while it insists that AI isn’t to blame, it also convened an internal meeting to discuss this specific issue, and The Financial Times reported that Amazon now requires junior and mid-level engineers to get sign off on AI-assisted changes to code. However you may feel about Amazon as a service, its engineers are likely indicative of corporate engineering on some level, which is making me wonder if we’re not going to have some real problems in software development in the next few years as a result.

What does the software industry look like if nobody is actually reading their code? How many software engineers are comfortable doing this? I’m sure somebody will read this and get terribly offended, but to be clear, I’m not accusing you of copy-pasting code you can’t understand and being happy if it works unless that’s exactly what you’re doing. 

To be explicit, allowing LLM to write all of your code means that you are no longer developing code, nor are you learning how to develop code, nor are you going to become a better software engineer as a result.

This isn’t even an insult or hyperbole. If you are just a person looking at code, you are only as good as the code the model makes, and as Mo Bitar recently discussed, these models are built to galvanize you, glaze you, and tell you that you’re remarkable as you barely glance at globs of overwritten code that, even if it functions, eventually grows to a whole built with no intention or purpose other than what the model generated from your prompt. 

I’m sure there are software engineers using these models ethically, who read all the code, who have complete industry over it and use it like a glorified autocomplete. I’m also sure that there are some that are just asking it to do stuff, glancing at the code and shipping it. It’s impossible to measure how many of each camp there are, but hearing Spotify’s CEO say that its top developers are basically not writing code anymore makes me deeply worried, because this shit isn’t replacing software engineering at all — it’s mindlessly removing friction and putting the burden of “good” or “right” on a user that it’s intentionally gassing up.

Ultimately, this entire era is a test of a person’s ability to understand and appreciate friction. 

Friction can be a very good thing. When I don’t understand something, I make an effort to do so, and the moment it clicks is magical. In the last three years I’ve had to teach myself a great deal about finance, accountancy, and the greater technology industry, and there have been so many moments where I’ve walked away from the page frustrated, stewed in self-doubt that I’d never understand something.

I also have the luxury of time, and sadly, many software engineers face increasingly-deranged deadlines set by bosses that don’t understand a single fucking thing, let alone what LLMs are capable of or what responsible software engineering is. The push from above to use these models because they can “write code faster than a human” is a disastrous conflation of “fast” and “good,” all because of…

I also want to be clear that I am extremely nervous about how many people appear to be fine with not reading code. I am currently (very slowly) learning Python, and every new thing I learn reinforces my overwhelming anxiety that there is a lot of software being written today by people who don’t read the output from LLMs and, in some cases, may not have understood it if they did. While I’m not saying all or even many software engineers might do this, I am alarmed by the idea that it’s becoming more commonplace — and even more alarmed that the reaction appears to be “ah it’s fine who gives a shit, it works.” 

Guess what! It doesn’t always work. Amazon Web Services had multiple recent outages caused by use of its Kiro AI coding tool, and while it insists that AI isn’t to blame, it also convened an internal meeting to discuss this specific issue, and The Financial Times reported that Amazon now requires junior and mid-level engineers to get sign off on AI-assisted changes to code. external.description:
Meta's Frontier Model Delay Exposes the Gap Between AI Spending and AI Readiness

Meta has pushed back the release of its next flagship AI model — internally codenamed Avocado — to at least May, after internal evaluations showed it trailing the current capabilities of competitors. This comes despite the company committing up to $135 billion in AI spending this year and a $14.3 billion bet on new leadership to accelerate its frontier model ambitions.

The detail worth sitting with isn't the delay itself — it's that Meta is now reportedly weighing whether to temporarily license a competitor's model to keep its AI products functional in the interim. For a company publicly chasing superintelligence, that's a significant signal about the distance between infrastructure investment and actual model performance.

For enterprise leaders, this is a useful reminder that capital and compute don't automatically translate into capability. The AI race isn't just being won by who spends the most — it's being won by who can close the gap between what a model promises in benchmarks and what it reliably delivers in production. 

That gap is where most enterprise AI strategies quietly fail too.

record.text:
If your governance framework only works when you deploy AI slowly, you're not governing—you're rationing. 

Check out this post to learn more: tinyurl.com/455c5mcc
#EnterpriseAI #SystemDesign #DeveloperExperience #AI

external.description:
📐 Arclio Blueprints | Why Governance Frameworks Break at Scale

Most AI governance frameworks have policies. They lack enforcement mechanisms that scale.

Under real operational pressure, governance fatigue sets in fast: review meetings multiply faster than teams can staff them, approval cycles stretch from days to weeks, and exceptions become the norm because the process can't keep pace with deployment velocity. Teams start routing around governance entirely or building shadow systems to stay productive.

These aren't compliance problems. They're process design failures in production.

What governance at scale requires:
- Automated policy enforcement that operates at the system level, not the committee level
- Risk-based tiering that applies heavy oversight where it matters and stays out of the way where it doesn't
- Built-in audit trails that generate compliance documentation as a byproduct of normal operations, not a separate workstream

The gap isn't stricter rules. It's governance infrastructure that can operate at the speed of development.

Most AI governance frameworks have policies. They lack enforcement mechanisms that scale.

Dear Judge Kaplan:
I am writing, as the holder of power of attorney for Sam Bankman-Fried, to request an extension of time to respond to the government's reply brief on his Rule 33 motion. He is filing the papers related to the Rule 33 motion prose. In addition to being currently incarcerated, without access to any word processing capabilities or most of his own files, he is being relocated from Terminal Island sometime in the next couple of weeks. While in transit, he will be out of contact with the outside world for some period of time that is hard to predict at this point. It depends on where he is being sent and how direct the transport is.
If you are willing to grant him an extension until April 1st-three weeks from yesterday-to file his response, it will probably cover most eventualities. If it doesn't, I will update the court as soon as I have new information.
Thank you for your consideration.
But?
Barbara H. Fried

Case 1:22-cr-00673-LAK Document 589 Filed 03/16/26 Page 2 of 2
Memorandum Endorsement
United States v. Bankman-Fried, 22-cт-0673 (LAK)
Ms. Fried is not a member of the Bar of this Court, has not sought leave to appear
pro hac vice, and has not filed an appearance. A power of attorney granted by the defendant does not authorize her to seek relief from the Court or otherwise to participate in this litigation.
Pridgen v. Andresen, 113 F.3d 391, 392-93 (2d Cir. 1997).
The Court of course understands that Ms. Fried is the defendant's mother, was
trained and practiced as a lawyer, and has taught at Stanford Law School. Nevertheless, with no disrespect, she lacks standing to file papers or seek relief in this case. Indeed, Bankman-Fried is represented in this matter, to this day, by three members ofthe Bar who have appeared here and who do have standing to act on his behalf.

Bankman-Fried's Bid for New Trial Hits Headwinds Before
'Confused' Second Circuit
BY ALYSSA AQUINO
SHAPIRO Arato Bach's efforts to redo Sam Bankman-Fried's cryptocurrency fraud trial hit a snarl in the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit, where all three panelists on Tuesday expressed confusion over the defense's arguments that vital evidence was kept out of the trial.
Alexandra Shapiro argued to an appeals panel that U.S. District
VICTOR J. BLUE/BLOOMBERG
Sam Bankman-Fried's attorney, Alexandra Shapiro, appeared to be hard-pressed to explain to a skeptical three-judge panel how her client's trial was unfair.
COURTESY PHOTO
Judge Lewis Kaplan had biased the trial of Bankman-Fried—who was later sentenced to 25 years in jail on multiple fraud and conspiracy counts and ordered to forfeit a gargantuan $11 billion-by refusing to let Bankman-Fried testify to jurors that attorneys were involved in the creation of certain bank accounts.
But Shapiro faced difficult questioning from a seemingly unconvinced three-judge panel whose members frequently interrupted the attorney to question how Kaplan had erred, when Bankman-Fried hadn't brought an advice of counsel defense and the attorneys' involvement was limited.
"The problem Judge Kaplan faced is he was as confused as we are on whether this was an advice of counsel defense," said Circuit Judge Barrington Parker Jr.
Shapiro argued that the excluded evidence would have gone towards arguments that Bankman-Fried had acted in good faith. But that argument didn't fare well with Parker.
Are you "seriously suggesting" that if Bankman-Fried had been able to testify there were attorneys involved in the drafting of corporate documents, "that the not-guilties would have rolled in?
On this record?" Parker asked.
His co-panelistsCircuit Judge
Eunice Lee and Circuit Judge Maria
Araujo Kahn-pointed
» Page 6

SBF
« Continued from page 1
out that Bankman-Fried had testified under oath that he didn't discuss the charged conduct with lawyers. In denying the testimo-ny, Kaplan found that the attorneys had "drafted plain vanilla legal documents" and putting that towards jurors would have implied that the attorneys had full knowledge of the facts.
From October to November 2023, Kaplan oversaw a four-week trial against Bankman-Fried, accused by Manhattan federal prosecutors of stealing more than $8 billion from customers of his cryptocurrency exchange
FTX.com.
Prosecutors argued that despite holding out FT as a safe place to deposit their money, Bankman-Fried secretly diverted billions of dollars of customer deposits to his cryptocurrency trading firm, Alameda Research.
There, prosecutors claimed, Bankman-Fried spent the FTX deposits on Alameda's expens-es, speculative investments and to enrich himself. FTX later declared bankruptcy in November
2022, after customers attempted to withdraw more money than what the exchange had on hand.
In challenging the guilty ver-dict, Shapiro argued Kaplan made several evidentiary and procedural rulings that prevented Bankman-Fried from putting up a strong case, including that Kaplan gave a faulty juror instruction on intent to defraud and wrongly barred evidence of Bankman-Fried's alleged belief that there were enough assets to cover customer withdrawals.
But the appeals panel appeared skeptical, with Parker observing as a whole that you won some things, you lost some things" in the trial.
"It seems at times you're spending more time wrangling Judge Kaplan than on the merits," he said.
Shapiro disagreed with that characterization.
While the panel put Shapiro through her paces, the jurists appeared receptive to her arguments against the $11 billion forfeiture order.
"What legitimate penal purpose does an $11 billion forfeiture serve?" Parker asked Assistant
U.S. Attorney Nathan Rehn.
Rehn said the forfeiture order was structured to be used to …

Es gibt ein Artefakt namens „Branch of Reincarnation“, das einem die Talente früherer Leben verleihen kann – allerdings nur, wenn man sich damit selbst die Kehle aufschlitzt. Der Teenager Touya Senji leidet unter geringem Selbstwertgefühl, weil er immer im Schatten seines begabten großen Bruders stand. Obwohl er in landesweiten Probeklausuren regelmäßig unter die Top 100 kommt, fühlt sich selbst das wie ein leeres Versprechen an. Als Touya zufällig miterlebt, wie seine Mitschülerin Haito Luo Buffett als „Returner“ mit den Fähigkeiten ihres früheren Lebens gegen einen Serienkiller kämpft, gerät sein Leben schlagartig in eine gefährliche neue Realität.
